en.extract:
Wallace's fruit dove (Ptilinopus wallacii) is a species of a bird in the pigeon family Columbidae. The name commemorates the British naturalist Alfred Russel Wallace. It is a rather large, long-tailed fruit dove with a length of 24–28 cm (9.4–11.0 in) and has been described as "one of the most beautiful" fruit doves. The forehead and crown are dull crimson, the lower face and throat are white, and the rest of the head, breast, neck, and upper back are pale bluish-grey. The wings and lower back are green and the belly is orange, separated from the chest by a white band. Both sexes look similar, but females have less extensive red on the head and a greenish tinge to their grey parts.
Endemic to Indonesia, Wallace's fruit dove is found in lowland riverine and coastal forests in the Lesser Sunda Islands, the Moluccas, the Aru Islands, and occasionally in southwestern New Guinea. Its diet consists of small fruits and berries. Nests are made out of twigs in branches and the only observed nest was made in November. Common to moderately common throughout most of its range, the Wallace's fruit dove is evaluated as being of least concern on the IUCN Red List.


Taxonomy and systematics
Wallace's fruit dove was described as Ptilonopus Wallacii by the English ornithologist George Robert Gray in 1858 on the basis of specimens from the Aru Islands acquired by the British naturalist Alfred Russel Wallace. The species' generic name comes from the Ancient Greek ptilon (feather) and pous (foot), while the specific name wallacii is in honour of Alfred Wallace. Wallace's fruit dove is the official common name designated by the International Ornithologists' Union. Other common names for the species include Wallace's fruit pigeon, Wallace's green fruit dove, golden-fronted fruit dove, yellow-fronted fruit dove, golden-shouldered fruit dove, and crimson-capped fruit dove. It has no subspecies.
Wallace's fruit dove is one of over 50 species in the fruit dove genus Ptilinopus. A 2014 study of mitochondrial and nuclear DNA by Alice Cibois and colleagues found that Wallace's fruit dove was most closely related to a clade formed by the orange-fronted and ornate fruit doves. This group is sister to the pink-spotted fruit dove and these four species are in turn sister to the grey-headed fruit dove. These five species are sister to a clade formed by the orange-bellied, white-headed, and claret-breasted fruit doves. The Tanna fruit dove is sister to all other species in the group. The following cladogram shows the relationships of the white-headed fruit dove with other species in its group based on the 2014 study:


Description

Wallace's fruit dove is a large, long-tailed fruit dove with a length of 24–28 cm (9.4–11.0 in). It has been described as "one of the most beautiful" fruit doves. The forehead and crown are dull crimson, sometimes extending to the back of the neck, and the lower face and throat are white. The rest of the head, neck, breast, and upper mantle are pale bluish-grey, separated from the orange belly patch by a white band. The shoulder patch is golden-bronze, while the mantle and inner wing coverts are bluish-grey with yellow edges that give a scaled appearance. The primaries and secondaries are dark shiny green, the latter having narrow yellow edges, while the  back, rump, and uppertail coverts are yellowish-green. The flanks and vent are mixed pale yellow and green. The central tail feathers green with a whitish band at the end, while the outer ones are darker with a greyish band. The iris is light red with a yellow to green inner ring, the orbital skin is bluish, and the feet are purplish to pinkish. The bill is yellow-greenish yellow with a paler tip. Females are similar to males, but have a greenish tinge to the grey on the neck and breast and a less intense orange belly patch. Juveniles have green edging on the crown, yellow-tipped green feathers on the mantle and wing coverts, and a green wash on the breast and mantle.
The beautiful fruit dove (P. pulchellus) looks similar, but is smaller, more compact, and has a shorter tail, with a purple instead of white band, less extensive red on the head, and orange-yellow undertail coverts. The upperparts are unmarked deep green. The rose-crowned fruit dove (P. regina) is also similar, but is smaller and more compact with a less extensive pink or pale greyish-purple crown patch bordered with yellow, a pale purple patch separating the breast and belly, rusty-orange undertail coverts, and a well-marked yellow band on the tail.
The species is usually silent, but has been recorded giving a loud, melancholy oooo... ooo ooo ooo, with the longest pause between the first and second note. A short woo is also made in excitement. A pigeon on Tanimbar presumed to be from this species was recorded making a repeated hooooow-huwuu, hoooow-huwuu, with the second note slightly shorter and higher-pitched.


Distribution and habitat
Wallace's fruit dove is found on the Babar Islands and Tanimbar Islands in the Moluccas, on the Banda Islands, Kur, Manggur, Taam, Komeer, Bacar, Tual, and Kai Kecil in the Lesser Sundas, and the Aru Islands. On New Guinea, individuals have infrequently been recorded in the southwest from the Mimika River to the Noord River, but these are thought to have been vagrants from the Aru Islands.
The species inhabits lowland forests near rivers and the coast, including mangroves, forest edges, savanna, gallery forests, and monsoon forests. It has also been recorded on islands where nearly all old-growth forest has been replaced with cultivation and appears to be able to adapt to secondary growth. On Kai Besar, it has been recorded from sea level up to elevations of 250 m (820 ft).


Behaviour and ecology
Wallace's fruit dove is frequently seen alone or in pairs, although it is a social fruit dove and forms flocks of 5–26 birds. On Tanimbar, it is easily noticeable, often flying over roads and clearings or perching in open trees. Its generation length (average age of parents in the current population) was reported as being 3.2 years in 2016.
The species feeds on small fruit and berries, plucking these directly from branches. It makes a flimsy nest out of twigs in tree branches. One observed nest was made in November.


Status
Wallace's fruit dove is listed as being of least concern by the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) on the IUCN Red List due to its sufficiently large range and lack of significant population decline. It is fairly common on Tanimbar and the Aru Islands and moderately common on the Kai Islands. It was not seen on the Banda Islands and Taam, where it was formerly common, in 1971. Its survival on highly populated islands indicates an ability to adjust to disturbed habitats.


References


External links

'''Wallace's fruit dove''' ('''''Ptilinopus wallacii''''') is a [[species]] of a bird in the pigeon family [[Columbidae]]. The name commemorates the British naturalist [[Alfred Russel Wallace]]. It is a rather large, long-tailed fruit dove with a length of {{Convert|24–28|cm|in|abbr=on}} and has been described as "one of the most beautiful" fruit doves.<ref name=":1" /> The forehead and {{Birdgloss|crown}} are dull crimson, the lower face and throat are white, and the rest of the head, breast, neck, and upper back are pale bluish-grey. The wings and lower back are green and the belly is orange, separated from the chest by a white band. Both sexes look similar, but females have less extensive red on the head and a greenish tinge to their grey parts.

[[Endemism|Endemic]] to Indonesia, Wallace's fruit dove is found in lowland riverine and coastal forests in the [[Lesser Sunda Islands]], the [[Moluccas]], the [[Aru Islands]], and occasionally in southwestern [[New Guinea]]. Its diet consists of small fruits and berries. Nests are made out of twigs in branches and the only observed nest was made in November. Common to moderately common throughout most of its range, the Wallace's fruit dove is evaluated as being of [[Least-concern species|least concern]] on the [[IUCN Red List]].

== Taxonomy and systematics ==
Wallace's fruit dove was [[Species description|described]] as ''Ptilonopus Wallacii'' by the English [[ornithologist]] [[George Robert Gray]] in 1858 on the basis of specimens from the [[Aru Islands Regency|Aru Islands]] acquired by the British naturalist [[Alfred Russel Wallace]].<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Zoological Society of London|url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/32271697#page/251/mode/1up|title=A list of the birds, with descriptions of new species, obtained by Mr. Alfred R. Wallace in the Aru and Ké Islands|last2=Gray|first2=George R.|series=[[Proceedings of the Zoological Society of London]]|publisher=[[Academic Press]]|year=1858|location=London|page=185|language=en}}</ref> The species' [[Generic name (biology)|generic name]] comes from the [[Ancient Greek]] ''ptilon'' (feather) and ''pous'' (foot), while the [[Specific name (zoology)|specific name]] ''wallacii'' is in honour of Alfred Wallace.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Jobling|first=James A.|title=Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names|publisher=[[Christopher Helm]]|year=2010|isbn=978-1-4081-2501-4|location=London|pages=322, 406|language=en}}</ref> Wallace's fruit dove is the official [[common name]] designated by the [[International Ornithologists' Union]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|editor-last=Gill|editor-first=Frank|editor2-last=Donsker|editor2-first=David|editor3-last=Rasmussen|editor3-first=Pamela|title=Pigeons|url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/pigeons/|access-date=2022-01-25|website=IOC World Bird List|doi=10.14344/IOC.ML.12.1|doi-access=free}}</ref> Other common names for the species include Wallace's fruit pigeon, Wallace's green fruit dove, golden-fronted fruit dove, yellow-fronted fruit dove, golden-shouldered fruit dove, and crimson-capped fruit dove.<ref name=":2">{{Cite book|last1=Beehler|first1=Bruce|title=Birds of New Guinea: Distribution, Taxonomy, and Systematics|last2=Pratt|first2=Thane|publisher=[[Princeton University Press]]|year=2016|isbn=978-1-4008-8071-3|location=[[Princeton, New Jersey]]|page=81|language=en|oclc=936447561}}</ref> It has no subspecies.<ref name=":0" />

Wallace's fruit dove is one of over 50 species in the fruit dove genus ''[[Ptilinopus]]''.<ref name=":0" /> A 2014 study of [[Mitochondrial DNA|mitochondrial]] and [[nuclear DNA]] by Alice Cibois and colleagues found that Wallace's fruit dove was most closely related to a [[clade]] formed by the [[Orange-fronted fruit dove|orange-fronted]] and [[Ornate fruit dove|ornate fruit doves]]. This group is [[Sister group|sister]] to the [[pink-spotted fruit dove]] and these four species are in turn sister to the [[grey-headed fruit dove]]. These five species are sister to a clade formed by the [[Orange-bellied fruit dove|orange-bellied]], [[White-headed fruit dove|white-headed]], and [[Claret-breasted fruit dove|claret-breasted fruit doves]]. The [[Tanna fruit dove]] is [[Sister group|sister]] to all other species in the group. The following [[cladogram]] shows the relationships of the white-headed fruit dove with other species in its group based on the 2014 study:<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Cibois|first1=Alice|last2=Thibault|first2=Jean-Claude|last3=Bonillo|first3=Céline|last4=Filardi|first4=Christopher E.|last5=Watling|first5=Dick|last6=Pasquet|first6=Eric|date=2014|title=Phylogeny and biogeography of the fruit doves (Aves: Columbidae)|url=https://linkinghub.elsevier.com/retrieve/pii/S1055790313003412|journal=[[Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution]]|language=en|volume=70|pages=442–453|doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2013.08.019|pmid=24012584|bibcode=2014MolPE..70..442C |url-access=subscription}}</ref>

== Description ==
[[File:PtilonopusWallaciiWolf.jpg|thumb|1858 illustration of the species]]
Wallace's fruit dove is a large, long-tailed fruit dove with a length of {{Convert|24–28|cm|in|abbr=on}}. It has been described as "one of the most beautiful" fruit doves.<ref name=":1" /> The forehead and {{Birdgloss|crown}} are dull crimson, sometimes extending to the back of the neck, and the lower face and throat are white. The rest of the head, neck, breast, and upper {{Birdgloss|mantle}} are pale bluish-grey, separated from the orange belly patch by a white band. The shoulder patch is golden-bronze, while the mantle and inner {{Birdgloss|wing coverts}} are bluish-grey with yellow edges that give a scaled appearance. The {{Birdgloss|primaries}} and {{Birdgloss|secondaries}} are dark shiny green, the latter having narrow yellow edges, while the  back, {{Birdgloss|rump}}, and {{Birdgloss|uppertail coverts}} are yellowish-green. The {{Birdgloss|flanks}} and {{Birdgloss|vent}} are mixed pale yellow and green. The central tail feathers green with a whitish band at the end, while the outer ones are darker with a greyish band. The [[Iris (anatomy)|iris]] is light red with a yellow to green inner ring, the [[Orbit (anatomy)|orbital]] skin is bluish, and the feet are purplish to pinkish. The bill is yellow-greenish yellow with a paler tip. Females are similar to males, but have a greenish tinge to the grey on the neck and breast and a less intense orange belly patch. Juveniles have green edging on the crown, yellow-tipped green feathers on the mantle and wing coverts, and a green wash on the breast and mantle.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":3" />

The [[beautiful fruit dove]] (''P. pulchellus'') looks similar, but is smaller, more compact, and has a shorter tail, with a purple instead of white band, less extensive red on the head, and orange-yellow undertail coverts. The {{Birdgloss|upperparts}} are unmarked deep green. The [[rose-crowned fruit dove]] (''P. regina'') is also similar, but is smaller and more compact with a less extensive pink or pale greyish-purple crown patch bordered with yellow, a pale purple patch separating the breast and belly, rusty-orange undertail coverts, and a well-marked yellow band on the tail.<ref name=":1" />

The species is usually silent, but has been recorded giving a loud, melancholy ''oooo... ooo ooo ooo'', with the longest pause between the first and second note. A short ''woo'' is also made in excitement. A pigeon on Tanimbar presumed to be from this species was recorded making a repeated ''hooooow-huwuu, hoooow-huwuu'', with the second note slightly shorter and higher-pitched.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":3" />

== Distribution and habitat ==
Wallace's fruit dove is found on the [[Babar Islands]] and [[Tanimbar Islands]] in the [[Moluccas]], on the [[Banda Islands]], [[Kur Island|Kur]], Manggur, Taam, Komeer, Bacar, Tual, and [[Kai Kecil]] in the [[Lesser Sundas]], and the [[Aru Islands Regency|Aru Islands]]. On [[New Guinea]], individuals have infrequently been recorded in the southwest from the [[Mimika River]] to the Noord River, but these are thought to have been [[Vagrancy (biology)|vagrants]] from the Aru Islands.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last1=Gibbs|first1=David|title=Pigeons and Doves: A Guide to the Pigeons and Doves of the World|last2=Barnes|first2=Eustace|last3=Cox|first3=John|publisher=[[Pica Press]]|year=2001|isbn=978-1-8734-0360-0|location=|pages=476–477|language=en|oclc=701718514}}</ref><ref name=":2" />

The species inhabits lowland forests near rivers and the coast, including [[mangrove]]s, [[forest edge]]s, [[savanna]], [[Gallery forest|gallery forests]], and [[Monsoon forest|monsoon forests]]. It has also been recorded on islands where nearly all [[old-growth forest]] has been replaced with cultivation and appears to be able to adapt to [[secondary growth]]. On [[Kai Besar]], it has been recorded from [[sea level]] up to elevations of {{Convert|250|m|ft|abbr=on}}.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":3">{{Cite journal|last1=Baptista|first1=Luis F.|last2=Trail|first2=Pepper W.|last3=Horblit|first3=H.M.|last4=Garcia|first4=Ernest|date=2020-03-04|editor-last=Billerman|editor-first=Shawn M.|editor2-last=Keeney|editor2-first=Brooke K.|editor3-last=Rodewald|editor3-first=Paul G.|editor4-last=Schulenberg|editor4-first=Thomas S.|title=Wallace's Fruit-Dove (''Ptilinopus wallacii'')|url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/wafdov1/1.0/introduction|language=en|publisher=[[Cornell Lab of Ornithology]]|doi=10.2173/bow.wafdov1.01|url-access=subscription|access-date=2022-02-01|journal=Birds of the World|s2cid=241204625}}</ref>

== Behaviour and ecology ==
Wallace's fruit dove is frequently seen alone or in pairs, although it is a [[Sociality|social]] fruit dove and forms flocks of 5–26 birds. On Tanimbar, it is easily noticeable, often flying over roads and clearings or perching in open trees.<ref name=":1" /> Its generation length (average age of parents in the current population) was reported as being 3.2 years in 2016.<ref name="iucn status 13 November 2021" />

The species feeds on small fruit and berries, plucking these directly from branches.<ref name=":1" /> It makes a flimsy nest out of twigs in tree branches.<ref name=":3" /> One observed nest was made in November.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Noske|first=Richard A.|date=2017|title=The dearth of information on the breeding habits of Wallacean birds, and why we should care about it|url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/319135129|journal=BirdingASIA|volume=27|page=28}}</ref>

== Status ==
Wallace's fruit dove is listed as being of [[Least-concern species|least concern]] by the [[International Union for Conservation of Nature]] (IUCN) on the [[IUCN Red List]] due to its sufficiently large range and lack of significant population decline.<ref name="iucn status 13 November 2021" /> It is fairly common on Tanimbar and the Aru Islands and moderately common on the [[Kai Islands]]. It was not seen on the Banda Islands and Taam, where it was formerly common, in 1971. Its survival on highly populated islands indicates an ability to adjust to [[disturbed habitat]]s.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":3" />

zh.extract:
金肩果鸠（学名：Ptilinopus wallacii），是鸽形目鸠鸽科的一种鸟类。


特征
金肩果鸠体重约208克，翼长约148.2毫米，嘴峰长约19.5毫米，喙宽度约4.7毫米，喙厚度约4.2毫米，跗蹠长约23毫米，尾长约72毫米。金肩果鸠在其分布区内为留鸟，其栖息在半开放的高大的树木组成的森林中，食性为草食性，主要食物来源是水果。


分布
新几内亚西南部的低地，南摩鹿加群岛，凯岛和阿鲁岛。


参考资料
